title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
Data centers have become essential infrastructure in the modern economy. As cloud computing, artificial intelligence, streaming services, and digital storage continue to expand, the demand for large-scale computing facilities has increased rapidly across the United States. New York State has experienced significant growth in data center development, particularly in and around major metropolitan areas such as New York City, Buffalo, Rochester, and Albany. While these facilities provide important technological and economic benefits, communities have increasingly raised questions about their potential local environmental and quality-of-life impacts.
One of the primary concerns surrounding data centers is their substantial energy demand. Large facilities require continuous electricity to operate servers and cooling systems, and many also rely on backup diesel generators for reliability during outages or peak demand periods. Critics argue that these facilities may contribute to localized pollution, increased strain on energy infrastructure, noise exposure, and other environmental burdens. Supporters, however, argue that modern data centers are generally cleaner than traditional industrial facilities and that many environmental concerns may be overstated.
This project examines whether areas located near data centers in New York State are associated with higher levels of PM2.5, a widely used measure of fine particulate air pollution. PM2.5 is an important environmental and public health indicator because exposure to elevated levels has been linked to respiratory and cardiovascular health risks. The central research question of this project is:
Are areas near data centers associated with higher PM2.5 levels?
To answer this question, I combine EPA air quality monitoring data, Census tract socioeconomic data, and geocoded data center locations throughout New York State. The analysis focuses on four metropolitan regions: New York City, Buffalo, Rochester, and Albany. I compare monitoring locations based on their proximity to the nearest data center while controlling for income, housing characteristics, population, industrial activity, and metro-level differences.
The empirical strategy primarily relies on regression analysis and geospatial distance measures. In addition, I incorporate a machine learning component using random forest models to explore whether more flexible predictive approaches identify similar patterns. The project is primarily descriptive and inferential rather than fully causal. While the analysis cannot definitively establish causation, it provides evidence regarding whether localized environmental differences are associated with proximity to data centers.
The results suggest that areas within one mile of a data center tend to have higher PM2.5 levels relative to areas located farther away, although the effect appears localized rather than widespread. The relationship becomes visible after controlling for socioeconomic and regional differences, highlighting the importance of accounting for confounding factors when evaluating environmental outcomes.
The broader contribution of this project is to provide a localized environmental analysis of data center proximity within New York State. Rather than arguing that data centers are inherently harmful, the goal is to better understand how these facilities may interact with surrounding communities and how policymakers and planners can manage future development responsibly.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
3.1 Data Sources
This project combines three primary datasets:

EPA Air Quality System (AQS) PM2.5 monitoring data
U.S. Census American Community Survey (ACS) tract-level socioeconomic data
Geocoded data center location data

The EPA AQS data provide daily PM2.5 observations for monitoring sites across New York State from 2020 through 2025. These observations are aggregated into yearly averages for each monitoring location.
Socioeconomic variables are obtained from the American Community Survey 5-year estimates. These variables include median household income, median home value, median rent, total population, and an industrial activity proxy.
Data center locations were collected from publicly available facility lists and geocoded using OpenStreetMap geocoding services. Geographic coordinates were used to calculate the distance from each monitoring location to the nearest data center.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
3.2 Unit of Observation
The primary unit of observation is a PM2.5 monitoring site-year observation. Each row in the final dataset represents the yearly average PM2.5 level observed at a monitoring location within a given year.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
3.3 Geographic Scope
The analysis focuses on four New York metropolitan regions:

New York City
Buffalo
Rochester
Albany

These metro areas were selected because they contain both PM2.5 monitoring infrastructure and significant concentrations of data center facilities.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
3.4 Key Variables

3.4.1 Outcome Variable

PM2.5 Mean: Yearly average PM2.5 concentration measured at EPA monitoring sites.



3.4.2 Main Explanatory Variables

Distance to Nearest Data Center: Continuous distance in miles between a monitoring site and the nearest data center.
Distance Bands: Categorical indicators representing:

Within 1 mile
1‚Äď3 miles
Over 3 miles




3.4.3 Control Variables

Median household income
Median home value
Median rent
Total population
Industrial activity share
Metro fixed effects

The industrial activity variable is constructed as the share of employed residents working in construction, manufacturing, transportation, warehousing, and utilities occupations. This variable serves as a proxy for underlying industrial intensity.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
3.5 Data Cleaning and Processing
The EPA PM2.5 files were imported and combined across years using R. Key variables such as coordinates, pollutant measurements, and dates were converted into consistent formats. Monitoring observations outside New York State were removed.
Data center addresses were cleaned and geocoded to obtain latitude and longitude coordinates. PM2.5 monitoring locations and Census tracts were converted into spatial objects using the sf package. Distances to the nearest data center were calculated using projected coordinate systems.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
3.6 Summary Statistics
Average PM2.5 levels vary across metro areas. New York City exhibits the highest average PM2.5 levels, while Albany and Rochester show lower average pollution levels.



Metro
Average PM2.5




Albany
6.78


Buffalo
7.34


NYC
8.89


Rochester
6.89



The descriptive statistics indicate substantial regional variation, highlighting the importance of controlling for metro-level differences in the empirical analysis.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
3.7 Data Limitations
Several limitations should be acknowledged. First, data centers are often located in already industrial or urbanized areas, making causal interpretation difficult. Second, precise facility-level opening dates and emissions data are not consistently available. Third, PM2.5 monitors are not evenly distributed geographically, which may introduce spatial coverage limitations.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
4.1 Regression Analysis
The primary empirical approach uses ordinary least squares (OLS) regression to estimate the relationship between PM2.5 and proximity to data centers.
The baseline specification is:
$$ PM2.5_i = _0 + _1 _i + _2 _i

_3 _i + _4 _i
_5 _i + _6 _i
_7 _i
_m + _i $$

Where:

\(PM2.5_i\) is the yearly average PM2.5 level
Distance variables measure proximity to data centers
\(X_i\) represents socioeconomic control variables
\(\gamma_m\) represents metro fixed effects
\(\varepsilon_i\) is the error term

The reference category for the distance-band analysis is locations more than three miles from a data center.
Metro fixed effects are included to control for baseline regional differences across NYC, Buffalo, Rochester, and Albany. This allows the analysis to focus on within-metro variation rather than broad statewide differences.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
4.2 Continuous Distance Model
A secondary specification replaces the categorical distance bands with a continuous distance variable:
$$ PM2.5_i = _0 + _1 _i

_2 _i + _3 _i
_4 _i + _5 _i
_6 _i
_m + _i $$

This specification tests whether PM2.5 changes gradually with distance.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
4.3 Machine Learning Component
In addition to regression analysis, I implement a random forest model to explore predictive relationships between PM2.5 and the explanatory variables.
The machine learning objective can be summarized as:
\[
\hat{PM2.5}_i = f(\text{Distance}_i, X_i)
\]
The random forest model uses an 80/20 train-test split. Model performance is evaluated using RMSE and MAE metrics. The machine learning component is primarily exploratory and intended to complement the regression analysis rather than replace it.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
4.4 Interpretation and Identification
This project is primarily descriptive and inferential rather than fully causal. While the regression models control for several important socioeconomic and regional factors, unobserved differences across locations may still influence the results. Therefore, the findings should be interpreted as evidence of association rather than definitive causal effects.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
5.1 Descriptive Findings
Raw average PM2.5 levels by distance band do not show substantially higher pollution levels near data centers. In some cases, locations within one mile even display slightly lower average PM2.5 levels than more distant areas.
However, these raw comparisons do not account for regional and socioeconomic differences.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
5.2 Regression Results
The regression analysis reveals a localized relationship between proximity to data centers and PM2.5.
Areas located within one mile of a data center exhibit higher PM2.5 levels relative to areas more than three miles away, after controlling for income, housing characteristics, population, industrial activity, and metro-level differences.
The effect is positive and marginally statistically significant, while the 1‚Äď3 mile category does not exhibit a meaningful relationship. This pattern suggests that any environmental association is concentrated very close to data center locations rather than spread across broader metropolitan regions.
The positive coefficient on median income likely reflects broader urban density effects. Higher-income observations in this dataset are often concentrated in dense urban areas such as New York City, which tend to have higher baseline pollution levels.
The inclusion of industrial activity controls helps address concerns that the estimated data center relationship simply reflects broader industrial land-use patterns.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
5.3 Continuous Distance Results
The continuous distance specification produces weaker results overall but maintains a negative relationship between distance and PM2.5. In other words, PM2.5 tends to decline as distance from data centers increases, although the relationship is not strongly statistically significant.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
5.4 Machine Learning Results
The random forest model does not identify distance as a dominant global predictor of PM2.5. Instead, broader socioeconomic and urban variables appear more important in explaining overall variation.
This finding is consistent with the regression analysis. The relationship between data center proximity and PM2.5 appears localized rather than broad enough to dominate predictive performance across all observations.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
5.5 Robustness and Interpretation
The consistency between the regression and machine learning results strengthens the broader interpretation of the project. Both approaches suggest that any relationship between data centers and PM2.5 is spatially concentrated rather than widespread.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
9.1 Additional Future Extensions
Potential future extensions include:

Pre/post analysis using facility opening years
Additional machine learning models such as XGBoost
Noise exposure analysis
Energy consumption and electricity infrastructure analysis
More detailed facility-level emissions data
Resident perception or community survey analysis

title: ggplot2 Basics
text:
This document introduces the ggplot2 workflow for building clear and professional visualizations in R.
You will learn how to:

create scatterplots, line plots, and bar charts
map variables to aesthetics (aes())
control color, size, transparency, and labels
use facet_wrap() to compare groups
polish plots with themes and annotations

title: ggplot2 Basics
text:
2.1 Install (one-time)


Show the code
# install.packages("tidyverse")

title: ggplot2 Basics
text:
2.2 Load packages


Show the code
library(tidyverse)

title: ggplot2 Basics
text:
5.1 Basic scatterplot


Show the code
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) +
  geom_point()

title: ggplot2 Basics
text:
5.2 Add transparency


Show the code
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5)

title: ggplot2 Basics
section: 5.3 Map color to a categorical variable
text:
5.3 Map color to a categorical variable


Show the code
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = displ, y = hwy, color = class)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7)

title: ggplot2 Basics
section: 5.4 Map size to a numeric variable
text:
5.4 Map size to a numeric variable


Show the code
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = displ, y = hwy, size = cyl)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6)









‚úÖ Rule of thumb:
- color = ... inside aes() means it changes by data values
- color = "blue" outside aes() means fixed color

title: ggplot2 Basics
text:
6.1 Add a smooth trend


Show the code
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth()

title: ggplot2 Basics
text:
6.2 Linear regression line


Show the code
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)

title: ggplot2 Basics
text:
8.1 Count bar chart


Show the code
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = class)) +
  geom_bar()

title: ggplot2 Basics
text:
8.2 Flip coordinates (nice for long labels)


Show the code
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = class)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip()

title: ggplot2 Basics
text:
8.3 Bar chart with fill color


Show the code
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = class, fill = drv)) +
  geom_bar()

title: ggplot2 Basics
text:
9.1 Step 1: Summarize with dplyr


Show the code
mpg_mean <- mpg |>
  group_by(class) |>
  summarise(mean_hwy = mean(hwy), .groups = "drop")

mpg_mean


# A tibble: 7 √ó 2
  class      mean_hwy
  <chr>         <dbl>
1 2seater        24.8
2 compact        28.3
3 midsize        27.3
4 minivan        22.4
5 pickup         16.9
6 subcompact     28.1
7 suv            18.1

title: ggplot2 Basics
text:
9.2 Step 2: Plot the summarized data


Show the code
ggplot(mpg_mean, aes(x = class, y = mean_hwy)) +
  geom_col()









‚úÖ geom_bar() counts rows automatically.
‚úÖ geom_col() uses your own y-values.

title: ggplot2 Basics
text:
10.1 Basic line plot


Show the code
ggplot(sales, aes(x = month, y = revenue)) +
  geom_line()

title: ggplot2 Basics
text:
10.2 Line + points


Show the code
ggplot(sales, aes(x = month, y = revenue)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point()

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
Data centers have become essential infrastructure in the modern economy. As cloud computing, artificial intelligence, streaming services, and digital storage continue to expand, the demand for large-scale computing facilities has increased rapidly across the United States. New York State has experienced significant growth in data center development, particularly in and around major metropolitan areas such as New York City, Buffalo, Rochester, and Albany. While these facilities provide important technological and economic benefits, communities have increasingly raised questions about their potential local environmental and quality-of-life impacts.
One of the primary concerns surrounding data centers is their substantial energy demand. Large facilities require continuous electricity to operate servers and cooling systems, and many also rely on backup diesel generators for reliability during outages or peak demand periods. Critics argue that these facilities may contribute to localized pollution, increased strain on energy infrastructure, noise exposure, and other environmental burdens. Supporters, however, argue that modern data centers are generally cleaner than traditional industrial facilities and that many environmental concerns may be overstated.
This project examines whether areas located near data centers in New York State are associated with higher levels of PM2.5, a widely used measure of fine particulate air pollution. PM2.5 is an important environmental and public health indicator because exposure to elevated levels has been linked to respiratory and cardiovascular health risks.
The central research question of this project is:

Are areas near data centers associated with higher PM2.5 levels?

To answer this question, I combine EPA air quality monitoring data, Census tract socioeconomic data, and geocoded data center locations throughout New York State. The analysis focuses on four metropolitan regions: New York City, Buffalo, Rochester, and Albany.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
Source and Scope
This project combines three primary datasets:

EPA Air Quality System (AQS) PM2.5 monitoring data
U.S. Census American Community Survey (ACS) tract-level socioeconomic data
Geocoded data center location data

The EPA AQS data provide daily PM2.5 observations for monitoring sites across New York State from 2020 through 2025. These observations are aggregated into yearly averages for each monitoring location.
Socioeconomic variables are obtained from the American Community Survey 5-year estimates. These variables include median household income, median home value, median rent, total population, and industrial activity measures.
Data center locations were collected from publicly available facility lists and geocoded using OpenStreetMap geocoding services.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
Variables

Outcome Variable

PM2.5 Mean: Yearly average PM2.5 concentration measured at EPA monitoring sites.



Main Explanatory Variables

Distance to nearest data center
Distance bands:

Within 1 mile
1‚Äď3 miles
Over 3 miles




Control Variables

Median household income
Median home value
Median rent
Total population
Industrial activity share
Metro fixed effects

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
Cleaning and Preparation
The EPA PM2.5 files were imported and combined across years using R. Variables such as coordinates, pollutant measurements, and dates were converted into consistent formats. Monitoring observations outside New York State were removed.
Data center addresses were cleaned and geocoded to obtain latitude and longitude coordinates. PM2.5 monitoring locations and Census tracts were converted into spatial objects using the sf package. Distances to the nearest data center were calculated using projected coordinate systems.
An industrial activity variable was created using the share of employed residents working in construction, manufacturing, transportation, warehousing, and utilities occupations.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
Exploratory Data Analysis
Average PM2.5 levels vary across metro areas.



Metro
Average PM2.5




Albany
6.78


Buffalo
7.34


NYC
8.89


Rochester
6.89



New York City exhibits the highest average PM2.5 levels, while Albany and Rochester display lower average pollution levels. These regional differences motivate the inclusion of metro fixed effects in the regression analysis.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
Modeling Goal
The primary modeling goal is to evaluate whether proximity to data centers is associated with PM2.5 levels while accounting for socioeconomic and regional differences.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
Models Used
This project uses multiple supervised learning approaches:

Linear regression
Random forest
Regression tree

Linear regression provides an interpretable baseline model, while random forest and regression tree models allow for nonlinear relationships and interactions among variables.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
Predictors and Outcome
The outcome variable is yearly average PM2.5.
Predictors include: - distance to nearest data center - distance bands - income - home value - rent - population - industrial activity - metro indicators

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
Empirical Regression Model
The baseline regression specification is:
\[\begin{aligned}
PM2.5_i =\ & \beta_0 \\
& + \beta_1 \text{Within1Mile}_i \\
& + \beta_2 \text{OneToThreeMiles}_i \\
& + \beta_3 \text{Income}_i \\
& + \beta_4 \text{HomeValue}_i \\
& + \beta_5 \text{Rent}_i \\
& + \beta_6 \text{Population}_i \\
& + \beta_7 \text{IndustrialActivity}_i \\
& + \gamma_m \\
& + \varepsilon_i
\end{aligned}\]
Metro fixed effects are included to account for baseline regional differences across NYC, Buffalo, Rochester, and Albany.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
Train/Test Split and Evaluation
The machine learning models use an 80/20 train-test split. Model performance is evaluated using RMSE and MAE metrics.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
Results and Interpretation
The regression analysis suggests that areas located within one mile of a data center tend to exhibit higher PM2.5 levels after controlling for socioeconomic and regional factors. The effect appears localized rather than widespread.
The continuous distance specification produces weaker results overall but maintains a negative relationship between distance and PM2.5, suggesting that pollution tends to decline as distance from data centers increases.
The random forest model identifies broader socioeconomic and urban variables as stronger global predictors of PM2.5 than distance alone. This supports the interpretation that the relationship between data centers and PM2.5 is localized rather than dominant across all observations.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
Clustering Approach
To complement the supervised models, I used k-means clustering to identify groups of monitoring locations with similar environmental and socioeconomic characteristics.
The clustering analysis included: - PM2.5 - distance to nearest data center - income - home value - rent - population - industrial activity
Variables were standardized before clustering to prevent differences in scale from dominating the results.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
Cluster Interpretation
The clustering analysis identified several distinct groups of monitoring locations.
One cluster consisted of more urban and industrial areas with higher PM2.5 levels and closer proximity to data centers. Another cluster represented lower-pollution areas located farther from data centers. A third cluster represented moderate-exposure locations with mixed socioeconomic characteristics.
The clustering results provide additional descriptive evidence that environmental exposure patterns vary meaningfully across monitoring areas.

title: Theodore Packert
text: Theodore Packert is a recent graduate from SUNY Geneseo.

text:
Education
State University of New York at Geneseo | Geneseo, NY  B.S. in Data Analytics | Aug 2022 - May 2026  Minor in Political Science

4.4 Interpretation and Identification
This project is primarily descriptive and inferential rather than fully causal. While the regression models control for several important socioeconomic and regional factors, unobserved differences across locations may still influence the results. Therefore, the findings should be interpreted as evidence of association rather than definitive causal effects.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
5.1 Descriptive Findings
Raw average PM2.5 levels by distance band do not show substantially higher pollution levels near data centers. In some cases, locations within one mile even display slightly lower average PM2.5 levels than more distant areas.
However, these raw comparisons do not account for regional and socioeconomic differences.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
5.2 Regression Results
The regression analysis reveals a localized relationship between proximity to data centers and PM2.5.
Areas located within one mile of a data center exhibit higher PM2.5 levels relative to areas more than three miles away, after controlling for income, housing characteristics, population, industrial activity, and metro-level differences.
The effect is positive and marginally statistically significant, while the 1‚Äď3 mile category does not exhibit a meaningful relationship. This pattern suggests that any environmental association is concentrated very close to data center locations rather than spread across broader metropolitan regions.
The positive coefficient on median income likely reflects broader urban density effects. Higher-income observations in this dataset are often concentrated in dense urban areas such as New York City, which tend to have higher baseline pollution levels.
The inclusion of industrial activity controls helps address concerns that the estimated data center relationship simply reflects broader industrial land-use patterns.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
5.3 Continuous Distance Results
The continuous distance specification produces weaker results overall but maintains a negative relationship between distance and PM2.5. In other words, PM2.5 tends to decline as distance from data centers increases, although the relationship is not strongly statistically significant.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
5.4 Machine Learning Results
The random forest model does not identify distance as a dominant global predictor of PM2.5. Instead, broader socioeconomic and urban variables appear more important in explaining overall variation.
This finding is consistent with the regression analysis. The relationship between data center proximity and PM2.5 appears localized rather than broad enough to dominate predictive performance across all observations.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
5.5 Robustness and Interpretation
The consistency between the regression and machine learning results strengthens the broader interpretation of the project. Both approaches suggest that any relationship between data centers and PM2.5 is spatially concentrated rather than widespread.

title: Living Near the Cloud: Quality of Life Near Data Centers in New York State
text:
9.1 Additional Future Extensions
Potential future extensions include:

Pre/post analysis using facility opening years
Additional machine learning models such as XGBoost
Noise exposure analysis
Energy consumption and electricity infrastructure analysis
More detailed facility-level emissions data
Resident perception or community survey analysis

title: Welcome To My Blog
text:
This is the first post in a Quarto blog. Welcome!

Since this post doesn‚Äôt specify an explicit image, the first image in the post will be used in the listing page of posts.

title: Starwars
text:
Let‚Äôs analyze the starwars data:
starwars <- read_csv("https://bcdanl.github.io/data/starwars.csv")

title: Starwars
text:
Variable Description for starwars data.frame
The following describes the variables in the starwars data.frame.

films List of films the character appeared in
name Name of the character
species Name of species
height Height (cm)
mass Weight (kg)
hair_color, skin_color, eye_color Hair, skin, and eye colors
birth_year Year born (BBY = Before Battle of Yavin)
sex The biological sex of the character, namely male, female, hermaphroditic, or none (as in the case for Droids).
gender The gender role or gender identity of the character as determined by their personality or the way they were programmed (as in the case for Droids).
homeworld Name of homeworld

title: Starwars
text:
Human vs.¬†Droid

ggplot(data = 
         starwars %>% 
         filter(species %in% c("Human", "Droid"))) +
  geom_boxplot(aes(x = species, y = mass, 
                   fill = species),
               show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "C")

title: Data Project
text:
‚ÄúTidy datasets are all alike, but every messy dataset is messy in its own way.‚ÄĚ ‚ÄĒ Hadley Wickham

R is a powerful language and environment for statistical computing and graphics. It is widely used among statisticians and data analysts for data analysis and developing statistical software. Here are some basic concepts and elements of R to help you get started:



Variables in R are used to store data. You can create a variable using the assignment operator <- (option/Alt + -). For example:


Code
my_variable <- 10


This will store the value 10 in my_variable.





R has several basic data types:

Numeric: For decimal values like 2.5.
Integer: For whole numbers like 2L (the L tells R it is an integer).
Character: For text or string values, e.g., "Hello".
Logical: For boolean values (TRUE or FALSE).






Vectors are a basic data structure in R. They contain elements of the same type. You can create a vector using the c() function:


Code
my_vector <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)






Data frames are used for storing data tables in R. It is a list of vectors of equal length. For example, to create a simple data frame:


Code
df <- data.frame(
  Name = c("Alice", "Bob"), 
  Age = c(25, 30)
  )






Functions are used to carry out specific tasks in R. For example, sum() is a function that adds numbers together:


Code
sum(1, 2, 3) # Returns 6


[1] 6






R has a vast collection of packages for various statistical tasks. You can install a package using install.packages("packageName") and load it using library(packageName).


Code
# install.packages("tidyverse")
library(tidyverse)






To get help on a specific function or topic, use the help() function or the shorthand ?, like ?sum on R Console.

title: Data Project
text:
Variables in R are used to store data. You can create a variable using the assignment operator <- (option/Alt + -). For example:


Code
my_variable <- 10


This will store the value 10 in my_variable.

title: Data Project
text:
R has several basic data types:

Numeric: For decimal values like 2.5.
Integer: For whole numbers like 2L (the L tells R it is an integer).
Character: For text or string values, e.g., "Hello".
Logical: For boolean values (TRUE or FALSE).

text:

Code
my_vector <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)

Code

title: Data Project
text:
Functions are used to carry out specific tasks in R. For example, sum() is a function that adds numbers together:


Code
sum(1, 2, 3) # Returns 6


[1] 6

title: Data Project
text:
R has a vast collection of packages for various statistical tasks. You can install a package using install.packages("packageName") and load it using library(packageName).


Code
# install.packages("tidyverse")
library(tidyverse)

title: Data Project
text: To get help on a specific function or topic, use the help() function or the shorthand ?, like ?sum on R Console.

title: Data Project
text:
2.1 Key Concepts

Data: The raw data that you want to plot.
aes() (Aesthetic Mappings): Defines how data are mapped to color, size, shape, and other visual properties.
Geoms (Geometric Objects): The type of objects that represent data points, like points, lines, bars, etc.
Facets: For creating small multiples, splitting data into subsets and displaying the same plot for each subset.
Scales: Control how data values are translated to visual properties.
Coordinate Systems: The plane in which data is plotted, e.g., Cartesian, polar.
Themes: Control the overall appearance of the plot, like background color, grid lines, and font sizes.

title: Data Project
text:
2.2 Examples
Let‚Äôs go through some examples to illustrate how ggplot can be used to create different types of visualizations.

2.2.1 Scatter Plot
Creating a scatter plot to explore the relationship between two variables, say mpg (miles per gallon) and wt (weight of the car) from the mtcars dataset.


Code
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) + 
  geom_point() +
  labs(x = "Weight of Car", y = "Miles Per Gallon",
       title = "Scatter plot of MPG vs Car Weight")









This code block creates a scatter plot where car weight is on the x-axis and miles per gallon on the y-axis. Each point represents a car.


2.2.2 Bar Chart
Creating a bar chart to show the count of cars by the number of cylinders.


Code
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = factor(cyl))) + 
  geom_bar() + 
  labs(x = "Number of Cylinders", y = "Count",
       title = "Count of Cars by Cylinders")









This plots a bar chart where each bar represents the count of cars with a certain number of cylinders.


2.2.3 Line Graph
Plotting a line graph, assuming we have a time series data.frame economics that is part of ggplot2 package.


Code
ggplot(economics, aes(x = date, y = unemploy)) + 
  geom_line() +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Number of Unemployed Persons",
       title = "Unemployment over Time") 









This code plots the unemployment numbers over time, with time on the x-axis and the number of unemployed persons on the y-axis.


2.2.4 Faceted Plot
Creating a faceted plot to compare scatter plots of mpg vs wt across different numbers of cylinders.


Code
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) + 
  geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(~cyl) +
  labs(title = "MPG vs Weight by Number of Cylinders")









This splits the data into subsets based on the number of cylinders and creates a scatter plot for each subset.

title: Data Project
text:
2.3 Conclusion
ggplot2 provides a powerful and flexible system for making a wide variety of plots. By understanding the grammar of graphics upon which it is based, you can build up complex visualizations from simple components, allowing for a deep and intuitive exploration of data.

title: dplyr Basics
text:
This document introduces the dplyr workflow for cleaning, transforming, and summarizing data in R.
You will learn how to:

select columns with select()
filter rows with filter()
sort data with arrange()
create new variables with mutate()
summarize data with summarise()
group operations with group_by()
combine datasets with joins

title: dplyr Basics
text:
2.1 Install (one-time)


Show the code
# install.packages("tidyverse")

title: dplyr Basics
text:
2.2 Load packages


Show the code
library(tidyverse)

title: dplyr Basics
text:

Show the code

title: dplyr Basics
dplyr Basics
text:
9.1 One summary value


Show the code
mpg |>
  summarise(mean_hwy = mean(hwy))


# A tibble: 1 √ó 1
  mean_hwy
     <dbl>
1     23.4

title: dplyr Basics
text:
9.2 Multiple summaries


Show the code
mpg |>
  summarise(
    mean_hwy = mean(hwy),
    sd_hwy   = sd(hwy),
    max_hwy  = max(hwy),
    min_hwy  = min(hwy)
  )


# A tibble: 1 √ó 4
  mean_hwy sd_hwy max_hwy min_hwy
     <dbl>  <dbl>   <int>   <int>
1     23.4   5.95      44      12


‚úÖ Tip: Use na.rm = TRUE if your data has missing values.

title: dplyr Basics
text:
10.1 Mean highway MPG by class


Show the code
mpg |>
  group_by(class) |>
  summarise(mean_hwy = mean(hwy), .groups = "drop") |>
  arrange(desc(mean_hwy))


# A tibble: 7 √ó 2
  class      mean_hwy
  <chr>         <dbl>
1 compact        28.3
2 subcompact     28.1
3 midsize        27.3
4 2seater        24.8
5 minivan        22.4
6 suv            18.1
7 pickup         16.9

title: dplyr Basics
text:
10.2 Count rows by group


Show the code
mpg |>
  group_by(drv) |>
  summarise(n = n(), .groups = "drop")


# A tibble: 3 √ó 2
  drv       n
  <chr> <int>
1 4       103
2 f       106
3 r        25

title: dplyr Basics
text:
15.1 left_join() (most common)


Show the code
students |>
  left_join(grades, by = "id")


# A tibble: 3 √ó 3
     id name  score
  <dbl> <chr> <dbl>
1   101 Alex     95
2   102 Bella    NA
3   103 Chris    88

title: dplyr Basics
text:

Show the code
students |>
  inner_join(grades, by = "id")

     id name  score

1   101 Alex     95
2   103 Chris    88

title: dplyr Basics
text:

  anti_join(grades, by = "id")

     id name 
  <dbl> <chr>
1   102 Bella
